markye:

I think i will have to try the merry edwards sauv blanc as it’s on at least half of the responses on here?

I would definitely get to one that’s a year or less from release, as I think it starts to lose its charm fairly quickly after that.

posts.name: Mark PACH
posts.cooked: While not wholly Sauvignon Blanc, I have been enjoying tremendously dry white Bordeaux - much more so than red Bordeaux on a dollar for dollar basis. You can have a very solid Graves white for <$40; while not daily drinker territory for most, I’m still a buyer at those prices. In fact, I’ve been enjoying it so much that I’m quickly displacing white Burgundy with white Bordeaux, especially on a risk-adjusted basis (premox-related).

Funny! Haven’t had a Rombauer in a long time, though it was one of the first wineries we visited when we moved up here in the mid-80s. The few times I’ve had the Merry Edwards SB, it comes across to me in a similar fashion: ripe and sweet, lacking the typicity of Sauvignon Blanc I find in other versions (mostly French, but there are some good examples made in California as well, like Rochioli in not too ripe years). Peay made a pretty nice SB this year.

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
Haut Brion Blanc is often more than 50% SB:

www.haut-brion.com:

Surface area planted: 2.87 hectares (7.09)

Planted grape varieties: 52.6% Semillon, 47.4% Sauvignon Blanc

Fermentation in oak casks

Length of initial maturing period in oak casks: 9-12 months in 50% new wood

2010: 46% Semillon, 54% Sauvignon Blanc
2012: 45% Semillon, 55% Sauvignon Blanc
etc

I don’t know what HB Blanc would taste like if you were to remove the Semillon.

But it’s about the only “Trophy” wine which I kinda regret that I’ll never be able to taste again, now that the deluge of fiat electrons in world markets has pushed its price beyond the stratosphere and into outer space.

Enjoyed Dagueneau, back in the day.

Never really understood Vatan - especially not vis-à-vis his Pinot Noir [which is one of the singular experiences in this entire hobby, if you ever get the chance].

Also, don’t forget New Zealand - Cloudy Bay ain’t exactly swill.

PS: Does Haut-Brion have surplus Semillon juice on a regular basis, or does Semillon just produce much lower crop yields than Sauvignon Blanc?

And if they have surplus Semillon, then what do they do with it?

posts.name: Jane Crabill
posts.cooked:
From Chavignol and/or Sancerre, The Loire another vote for Pascal or Francois Cotat, also Alphonse Mellot, and Lucien Crochet “Le Chene.” (I haven’t had the Vatan.)

From California: St. Supery and Joel Gott are reasonably priced and I have liked in past vintages but haven’t had any in the last couple years.

From New Zealand: Nobilo Icon, Cloudy Bay (in some vintages but maybe not worth the extra price), Seresin, Kono, the Crossings and Huia.

(I’m not a big fan of the Merry Edwards Sauvignon Blanc although I like many of her Pinot Noirs. She includes grapes from the Sauvignon Musque clone which make the wine a bit fuller or richer and more floral in its nose and not the crisp, clean style I personally prefer. However, she has developed a loyal following for this wine.)

posts.name: Nigel Groundwater
posts.cooked:
We are in the Loire every year so my experience and likes are somewhat biased.

I have never had the pleasure of a Sauvignon Blanc from the USA but have yet to find one from Down Under or South America that I prefer to the many Pouillys, Sancerres, Menetou-Salons, Valencays and good old Touraines that we buy and cellar every year.

Favourites include wines from the huge Henri Bourgeois range, Thomas Labaille and Francois Cotat - all Chavignol based although the latter requires an appointment while the others you can walk into and taste. Chavignol has the added attraction of having a good Brasserie and a Michelin starred restaurant, both in the Hotel Cote des Monts Damnes which is part of the Bourgeois family. Edmond Vatan’s exceptional Clos La Neore [made since 2008 by daughter Anne wife of Nady Foucault of Clos Rougeard] seems mainly destined for the USA but I usually find the odd bottle along with the Cotats’ range of wines in Francois Chidaine’s shop La Cave Insolite in Montlouis.

In Sancerre itself Alphonse Mellot and the Vacheron family have outlets where one can taste their range of excellent SBs [as well of course as their reds but that’s another grape/subject and equally true of most of the others already mentioned] and Pascal Cotat can also be found there.

For everyday drinking Andre Dezat makes an excellent value Sancerre and a pretty good Pouilly Fume.

The Dagueneau [Didier, now by his son & daughter] Pouilly and other wines are exceptional but are now only special treats for us since his death has only seen the already high prices continue upwards. Their [newer] Sancerre, from the Cotes Damnes IIRC, is good but already too expensive versus worthy already mentioned competitors in Chavignol. As with other difficult-to-get-at-source wines like the Cotats and Vatan we usually pick up the odd bottle of the basic Dagueneau Blanc Fume de Pouilly and, much more occasionally a Buisson Renard, a Pur Sang or a Silex at Chidaine’s shop.

From Sancerre we usually drive across to the Minchin’s, La Tour St. Martin, Crosses, Menetou-Salon to pick-up some of their oaked and un-oaked SBs including a Valencay cuvee plus some of their excellent reds. Henry Pelle’s SB Morogues, Menetou-Salon are good too but I have never tasted his Sancerre.

Closer to where we stay at the hotel/restaurant the Domaine des Hauts de Loire at Onzain the Barbous at the Domaine des Corbillieres at Oisly make a great value range of wines from a variety of grapes including oaked and unoaked SBs. Great-grandfather Fabel Barbou who bought the estate in 1923 was a key figure in rise of Touraine Sauvignon Blanc.

Nigel -

Next time you’re in the Loire, two I recommend you visit and try are:

  1. Menetou-Salon, Domaine de Chatenoy (Isabelle and Pierre Clément). http://www.clement-chatenoy.com/

2, Pouilly-Fumé “Cuvée Silice”, Domaine Francis Blanchet. http://www.vins-francis-blanchet.fr/

Cheers!

Bob

spottswoode
bedrock

Thank you Bob. I have taken note for my GPS in the car.

We will be in Chavignol and Sancerre at the end of July and later at the Minchins in Menetou-Salon so will try to take in the Domaine de Chatenoy.

If we go across the river from Sancerre we will look at Domaine Francis Blanchet too but our main reason for making the short journey across [Dagueneau] has disappeared with price inflation.

Nigel Groundwater:

If we go across the river from Sancerre we will look at Domaine Francis Blanchet too but our main reason for making the short journey across [Dagueneau] has disappeared with price inflation.

Blanchet’s Cuvée Silice is not exactly a replacement for Dagueneau’s Silex - but it’s not in the same exalted price category, either, generally retailing in the U’S. in the $20-25 range.

Why is it not a replacement for Silex? Because it is a traditional, prototypical Pouilly-Fumé, which Dagueneau’s wines were not. An excellent example of the appellation.

While I always admired Dagueneau’s wines, I did not seek them out. Price was one reason. Other reasons:

  • Despite being from the appellation, Dagueneau’s wines were never, at least to my palate, Pouilly-Fumé. They’re atypical. They’re sui generis. They’re Vin de Dagueneau. I prefer wines that more closely reflect their terroir.
  • The Dagueneau wines are brash. They mirror Didier Dagueneau’s larger-than-life personality. I tend to prefer wines that whisper over those that shout.

posts.name: J. Singleton
posts.cooked:
So just received my Windy Oaks order which included several whites. I couldn’t wait to try out the Sauv Blancs. Heeeeeeeell yes. I opened both the 2012 Santa Cruz and the Monterey bottlings. I preferred the Santa Cruz but both were fantastic. Crisp, clean, and acidic. Just how I like my SBs. And as a club member, $17.60 and $12.80 respectively… stupid good deal, especially the latter. These are worthy of case purchases without a second thought.



Also had the 2012 Cowan SB. This one seemed a bit shy on day one. Kind of soft and rounded and not giving up too much. But come day two, YES please! While still soft, favors were abundant and as the wine warmed up it got better. Wouldn’t want it room temperature but this should be served slightly warmer than most whites, in my opinion. Another good buy at $20.

posts.name: J. Singleton
posts.cooked: Anyone know why these pics seem blurry when uploaded? Taken on my iPad. Doesn’t look blurry like this.

posts.name: Steve Lagier
posts.cooked: 2nd for Selene!!!

posts.name: J. Singleton
posts.cooked: So thanks to some of you for recommending Quivet Sauv Blanc. Order arrived today and of course I couldn’t wait to open. No formal tasting notes, but most enjoyable. Very light in color, but quite flavorful. Very crisp, sleek, and refreshing. I didn’t do it justice by chilling then opening immediately, but it seems to taste better chilled than allowing it to slowly warm up. I’ll see if that holds true next week when I open another. Good stuff!

The only SA SB I’ve ever had was from Buitenverwachting. Excellent.

Buitenverwachting

WINES - Buitenverwachting

Rooted in deep, decomposed granite soils and cooled by the temperate sea breezes from the surrounding oceans, Buitenverwachting wines are an expression of this unique climate. The slow ripening climatic conditions with cold nights and modest day time...

Est. reading time: 7 minutes

It occurs to me now that I think we had an F. Cotat wine (Monts Damnes?) together that I thought was interesting, and not as I described my experiences with P. Cotat. posts.cooked:
Beast I’ve had recently? This is not a joke: 2015 Kirkland Signature [Costco house label] Marlborough “Ti Point.” $6.99, IIRC. Greengage plum, gooseberries. melon, freshly-mown Bermuda hay; crisp and utterly refreshing on the kind of sultry days we’ve been having lately; perfect to pair with Kirkland Signature fresh goat cheese.

Thanks for reviving this thread.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked: Just recently: a fabulous 2008 Comtesse by Gerard Boulay and a beautiful, “power-without-weight” 2012 Monts Damnes by Francois Cotat. I am a fan of Casa Marin, and Clos Floridene (If bordeaux blanc counts). Sean Minor also makes a decent SB for the ~$10 price point.

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked:
A quick CT search of Sauvignon Blanc between 2005-2015 picked up Kamen and Vineyard 29 with consistently high scores - for US made SB.
Bordeaux standouts included LMHB and HB of course, Chevalier and Smith Haut Lafitte.
I didn’t pay much attention to the majority of high scoring wines - where only a few bottles are listed in cellars. There’s a crapload listed at 95 point average.
I stopped looking on page 3 when the average score dropped below 95.
I’m sure I could narrow the search even more to highlight the readily available value wines.

My Sauvignon Blanc drinking experience is mostly limited to Merryvale & Starmont, and a couple of cheap white Bordeaux.
But it’s an area I’m getting ready to explore further.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Mike Grammer:

Funny, I’ve been thinking about this too, and I have to say my impression, based on the “young” wines I’ve tasted after the transition, is that there is a remarkable consistency both in terms of style and quality. We’ll see, I suppose.

posts.name: Darren Leininger
posts.cooked: Von Winning SB is amazing! I’ve had all three and they are, each in their own way, wonderful examples of SB. The II is fruity and approachable and is great with the sunset or a roast chicken. Think lemon meringue pie. The I is a bit more serious and my favorite of the three. Super intense SB character on the zingy, citrus side, think creamy, tart key lime pie, with great acidity and a mouth filling presence that goes on for quite some time. Simply wonderful. The 500 is a trip to the very serious side of SB. I think it needs many years to resolve but for now it is extremely slate-y, mineral-y and oily. Like a great GG but clearly SB instead of Riesling.

posts.cooked: I’m a big, big fan of the Alpha Estate SB out of Greece.

posts.name: Nolan E
posts.cooked:
Ed Gonzales:
Rolondo Mota:

Vineyard 29 …

$$$, but man it’s good.

I had the absolute pleasure of picking a portion of and making (but I’m not the ‘winemaker’) the 2015 Vineyard 29 Estate Blanc. A stunning wine and it almost offends me when people scoff at the price.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Nolan,

Why would it offend you? Pricing is not an absolute on any wine variety and the tendency is always going to be to compare.

That said, I’d love to try it - and find out why it’s priced at what it is and whether, to me, it’s worth the tariff.

And I wI’ll say that the pricing of ‘better’ domestic sauv Blancs appears to be in the rise in general.

Cheers.
My summer list includes (budget friendly) Vogelzang Res Happy Canyon, Bayten and Ventana. For many years enjoyed Vogelzang and recently the Ventana is very impressive.

posts.name: Nolan E
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:

Nolan,

Why would it offend you? Pricing is not an absolute on any wine variety and the tendency is always going to be to compare.

That said, I’d love to try it - and find out why it’s priced at what it is and whether, to me, it’s worth the tariff.

And I wI’ll say that the pricing of ‘better’ domestic sauv Blancs appears to be in the rise in general.

Cheers.

The response is usually something like ‘I wouldn’t pay that much for white wine’ which is annoying of itself and then people start quantifying by variety. Sort of like the Oregon Chardonnay thread, it starts to make sense when you compare specific wines to similarly priced bottles.

But it mainly offends me because they don’t realize what goes into the V29 Blanc (in terms fruit quality, low yields, quality of barrels, time in barrel, amount of attention paid, etc).

Tvrtko C.:
Mike Grammer:

Dagueneau, yes—the stuff Didier made.

Funny, I’ve been thinking about this too, and I have to say my impression, based on the “young” wines I’ve tasted after the transition, is that there is a remarkable consistency both in terms of style and quality. We’ll see, I suppose.

Whatever you do, don’t do a Vertical of the wines…then it’s crystal clear that they’re VERY different. Didier’s wines have more grace and poise…Louis Benjamin’s doing a good job, but in my thoughts…it hasn’t been worth the price increases.

posts.name: Curt Wood
posts.cooked:
Nolan-Sorry I offend you. Live on a fixed income and, unlike you I suppose, don’t have unlimited dollars to spend on wine. We have to pick and choose what we buy and consume. Your taste in SB must be terrific along with your income. My only hope is the lottery.

Cheers,
Curt

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Nolan E:
larry schaffer:

Nolan,

Why would it offend you? Pricing is not an absolute on any wine variety and the tendency is always going to be to compare.

That said, I’d love to try it - and find out why it’s priced at what it is and whether, to me, it’s worth the tariff.

And I wI’ll say that the pricing of ‘better’ domestic sauv Blancs appears to be in the rise in general.

Cheers.

The response is usually something like ‘I wouldn’t pay that much for white wine’ which is annoying of itself and then people start quantifying by variety. Sort of like the Oregon Chardonnay thread, it starts to make sense when you compare specific wines to similarly priced bottles.

But it mainly offends me because they don’t realize what goes into the V29 Blanc (in terms fruit quality, low yields, quality of barrels, time in barrel, amount of attention paid, etc).

I hear you and understand what you are saying.

Of course, the opposite can be said about wines as well - some do very little yet exist in an area, or have a reputation, that equates to higher bottle prices, regardless of what’s inside the bottle.

Pricing is something that always intrigues me. I’ve had some folks tell me that if I charged MORE for my wines, they’d purchase more of them. Seriously . . .

And in our industry, ‘inputs’ does not necessarily equate to higher prices. Yes, you can break out cost of grapes, new barrels, labor, etc and try to come up with pricing based on that, but if this happened everywhere, my guess is that some areas that currently have higher bottle prices would continue to ‘add on’, regardless of these ‘objective’ costs . . .

Cheers.

posts.name: RS Beck
posts.cooked:
Sancerre or Pouilly Fume

I like my Sauvignon Blanc acidic, steely, salty and chalky and I prefer that it not see any new oak.

I find that the most expensive Sauv Blancs are often more expensive because they’ve seen new oak, so I am more likely to steer away from them.

I don’t believe it is necessary to pay more than $30 to get great Sauvignon Blanc and it’s possible to get really good stuff around $20.

My current favorites are;

Sancerre

Morin Vieilles Vignes and Ovide
Lucien Crochet Sancerre & Le Chenes
Pascal and Nicolas Reverdy Le Coutes
Laporte Le Rochoy

Pouilly Fume

Regis Minet Veilles Vignes

posts.name: Nolan E
posts.cooked:
Curt Wood:

Nolan-Sorry I offend you. Live on a fixed income and, unlike you I suppose, don’t have unlimited dollars to spend on wine. We have to pick and choose what we buy and consume. Your taste in SB must be terrific along with your income. My only hope is the lottery.

Cheers,
Curt

I’ve never bought the V29 Blanc, I was fortunate enough to work there and get to drink it on occasion. I live on a very fixed income, likely at the bottom tier of posters here, but I’m not offended at the pricing of DRC, Mouton, Salon, etc. I just choose not to buy them as I’d rather pay rent.

I’m rather interested in wine and have chosen to try and make a career out of it, luckily I’ve worked for some great people and met some great people who are generous with their knowledge and resources.

I probably make as much as Markus thinks someone in the wine industry should.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Kirk.Grant:
Tvrtko C.:
Mike Grammer:

Dagueneau, yes—the stuff Didier made.

Funny, I’ve been thinking about this too, and I have to say my impression, based on the “young” wines I’ve tasted after the transition, is that there is a remarkable consistency both in terms of style and quality. We’ll see, I suppose.

Whatever you do, don’t do a Vertical of the wines…then it’s crystal clear that they’re VERY different. Didier’s wines have more grace and poise…Louis Benjamin’s doing a good job, but in my thoughts…it hasn’t been worth the price increases.

Kirk, I have never done a vertical, but I have had the wines very close together quite a number of times, and I still can’t say that I’ve noticed a dramatic difference in style or quality (then again, much as I like them, I am not sure that for me “grace” is necessarily one of the first words that spring to mind when describing Didier’s wines: in relative -SB- terms, I’ve always felt they were more about intensity and concentration, in a very good way). Also, there has been no appreciable price-hike at all here in Europe, at least where I buy from. Buisson has always been about 40-50 EUR retail, Pur Sang 50-60, and Silex 70-80 EUR. Still very much in that ballpark, if you know where to look.

Also, there has been no appreciable price-hike at all here in Europe, at least where I buy from. Buisson has always been about 40-50 EUR retail, Pur Sang 50-60, and Silex 70-80 EUR. Still very much in that ballpark, if you know where to look.

posts.name: RS Beck
posts.cooked:
Steve Brickley:

lucien crochet sancerre cul beaujeu

I think you may be confused.

I’m not aware of a Cul Beaujeu bottling by Lucien Crochet and I’ve not been able to find any reference to one.

Lucien Crochet makes a basic Sancerre, a Le Chene, and a Le Chene Marchand.

Producers that I know who make a Beaujeu bottling include Boulay, Pascal Cotat and Yves Martin. Perhaps you are thinking of one of these? There may be others.

posts.name: Steve Brickley
posts.cooked:
• 2012 Lucien Crochet Sancerre Le Cul de Beaujeu - France, Loire Valley, Upper Loire, Sancerre (2/1/2015)
    Nothing in the way with this wine. Drinks right out of the bottle with improvement over the next two hours. No reduction this young, lighter acid but enough to last a few years. Ever so slight wood. Yeast and lime on entry. Ever so slight sweetness, pear, tropical. Enjoyed by all, an easy summer or warm weather wine if a chill on it. Lovely, easy, bright, simple, fun. Got 3, went back for 6 more for summer fillers. Yum. (93 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Had a 2013 Matthieson last night. I think it’s 55% Sav Blanc then cut with some Semillon and a couple of other grapes. A rounder take on Sav Blanc, softened by the Semillon and some soft oak notes. Good acid, nice range of tropical fruits.

posts.name: RS Beck
posts.cooked:
Steve Brickley:
  • 2012 Lucien Crochet Sancerre Le Cul de Beaujeu - France, Loire Valley, Upper Loire, Sancerre (2/1/2015)
    Nothing in the way with this wine. Drinks right out of the bottle with improvement over the next two hours. No reduction this young, lighter acid but enough to last a few years. Ever so slight wood. Yeast and lime on entry. Ever so slight sweetness, pear, tropical. Enjoyed by all, an easy summer or warm weather wine if a chill on it. Lovely, easy, bright, simple, fun. Got 3, went back for 6 more for summer fillers. Yum. (93 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

Thank you. This is interesting! I love Lucien Crochet Sancerre so I’d be very interested in finding some. I find no reference to this bottling on their website. I recently wrote to K&L asking if they are going to be bringing in the 2014 Le Chene soon. K&L is my usual supplier for Lucien Crochet. K&L replied that they will not be bringing it in because they no longer work with LC’s distributor. I’m assuming they mean someone local because I can’t imagine that they mean they are no longer working with any wines from Rosenthal. There is nothing available in the US on Wine Searcher. I’m getting my Le Chene from Wine Library in NJ.

One other point; that label looks nothing like their other labels. It’s weird that there is no reference to this bottling on the Lucien Crochet site. Curious.

http://www.lucien-crochet.fr/accueil/index.en.php?rub=vins

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Rob,
I’ve had the Lucien Crochet CdB, and it’s fabulous, in a very rich style. I bought a few 2010s from Envoyer a few years ago, found another at K&L. Even emailed the domaine to ask about it. Got this info from Lucien:

This wine is very special and can’t be compared with our white wines as La Croix du Roy and Le Chêne. The grapes come from a plot called “Cul de Beaujeu” in the village of Chavignol, not Bué. The terroir is different with kimmerridgian marls ( “terres blanches” in french), and the wine is vinified in 600 liters oak barrels instead of stainles steel tanks. The production is very tiny, in 2010 we produced about 1800 bottles that’s the reason why you can’t find anymore Cul de Beaujeu in California. We try to share this small quantities between all our best clients, and try to make everyone happy.

This wine is dry, with about 4 grammes of residual sugar, and you are right, Sancerre wines are dry. You can keep the “Cul de Beaujeu” for about 10 years, in good conditions of storage.
Laurence Crochet

I may still have a bottle left, remind me next time we get together.

posts.name: Michel Davidoff
posts.cooked: VML - by far my favorite!

posts.name: RS Beck

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked: Les Montes Damnes. Several good producers to choose from.

I really love Merry Edwards but the price is tough for a weekday/daily drinker. Any recommendations for something similar but a bit cheaper? Just grabbed a Frogs Leap and Margerum SB to give them a whirl

posts.name: Ben B Floyd
posts.cooked: Chavignol bottlings from V Delaporte and Thomas-Labaille.

posts.name: Andrew Northup
posts.cooked: Marjan Simcic Opoka Sauvignon Blanc…favorite I’ve ever tasted

posts.cooked: Evan: you might want to try Hall, Provenance, Chalk Hill, Bedrock, Sbragia SB’s
